rho: Technical advice and regulated-product handling still require escalation and controls.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Warm Air Heating and Air-Conditioning Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product sales to trade contractors and dealers, supported by stocked local branches, delivery or pickup, technical support, product availability, credit, and territory or brand relationships; revenue is predominantly transaction-based product resale rather than a contractual recurring fee.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers' captive branches, pure equipment manufacturers, installation contractors, room-air-conditioner appliance wholesalers, hydronic-only wholesalers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent and owner-controlled merchant wholesalers repeatedly supplying warm-air heating, central air-conditioning, air-quality, parts, and installation supplies to external HVAC contractors and dealers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 423 durable-goods wholesale workforce rather than 423730 task hours: its largest roles include wholesale sales representatives, freight/material movers, shipping/receiving clerks, and delivery drivers. I map customer service, inside-sales support, quoting, order entry, purchasing, inventory planning, and back-office work to partial AI exposure, while treating physical handling, driving, relationship selling, and accountable technical judgment as much less exposed. The broader-subsector mix may overstate exposure if HVAC distribution is more technical and branch-intensive, or understate it if its quoting and product-data burden is unusually high.
caveats: The public BLS page used here reports NAICS 423, not the six-digit industry.
Exposure is wage-weighted judgment, not an observed automation rate.
Large public-distributor technology adoption may exceed that of LMM firms.
a.change_evidence: A 423730-specific OEWS occupation-by-wage extract combined with time-and-motion samples from distributor inside sales, counter sales, purchasing, warehouse, delivery, credit, and finance teams, coded at the task level for current automation, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is split between information-heavy commercial and administrative workflows and physical branch logistics. Watsco already uses e-commerce, pricing optimization, product-information systems, business intelligence, and AI, confirming that meaningful work can be digitized; the wage-weighted opportunity is nevertheless well below one-half because warehouse, delivery, technical support, and relationship work remain substantial.

d5.bridge: AHRI shipment units proxy wholesale quantity but cover selected central air conditioners and air-source heat pumps rather than the full constant-quality basket, and 2025 was distorted by the refrigerant transition. Watsco reports 80%-90% of U.S. residential equipment unit sales are replacement, 102 million installed systems were over ten years old, and equipment lives are roughly 8-20 years. I use the installed-base replacement evidence to look through the 2025 shipment decline and allow modest five-year real quantity growth. The result could be high if affordability or construction stays weak, and low if heat-pump adoption, climate, and replacement accelerate.
caveats: Shipment units are not distributor service-basket demand and omit many parts and supplies.
Weather, housing, interest rates, regulation, tariffs, and equipment affordability can dominate five-year outcomes.
Watsco's installed-base claims mix company estimates with cited third-party data.
d5.change_evidence: An AHRI five-year shipment series normalized for pre-buys and A2L transition, paired with EIA installed-stock age by system type, housing completions, repair-versus-replace rates, and distributor same-branch unit data, would materially move the estimate.
d5.rationale: Near-term unit shipments were sharply down, but the current service basket is supported by a large aging installed base, essential heating/cooling needs, parts demand, efficiency and refrigerant transitions, and new construction. On a constant-price and constant-quality basis, these drivers support roughly stable to modestly higher five-year quantity rather than extrapolating the transition-year decline.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may include entities whose true EBITDA is outside the band.
Territorial brand agreements and supplier concentration can make nominally independent firms less transferable.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the frozen 703 candidates classifying ownership, percent external-customer revenue, repeat-customer share, supplier agreement transferability, installation revenue, and owner dependence would materially tighten eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition itself is merchant wholesale distribution, and contractor customers repeatedly need equipment, replacement parts, supplies, inventory availability, and technical support. Most true independent LMM firms therefore fit the lens. Ineligible firms include captive or manufacturer-controlled branches, entities whose economics are primarily installation, thin trading operations without durable service relationships, and businesses too dependent on a nontransferable owner or supplier authorization.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423730 ($9,363,479,865) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423730 ($80,635,956,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $994,867 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423730); class <5: 762 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,949,840 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $221,697 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 392 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,049,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $574,130 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 290 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,139,445 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,039,155 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 413 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,561,673 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,361,162 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $155,342,377 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,662,428 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $607,104,031 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $69,027,728 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 703

o.bridge: Watsco's operating description shows both displacement and persistence: customers can self-serve search, pricing, ordering, status, scheduling, and payment online, yet the distributor maintains dense stocked warehouses, technical expertise, broad product access, credit, and time-sensitive fulfillment. This is same-industry evidence from a scaled operator, not a direct measure of year-five operator-required quantity. It may overstate persistence if manufacturers or marketplaces expand direct fulfillment, or understate it because LMM customers may require more local human help.
caveats: Operator requirement is distinct from human-touch requirement.
Manufacturer-owned distribution and direct digital channels are credible substitution risks.
The estimate assumes regulation and urgent fulfillment preserve local accountability.
o.change_evidence: Supplier channel-strategy interviews with major OEMs, distributor customer-order data by direct/e-commerce/branch channel, marketplace share, drop-ship rates, and contractor willingness-to-buy-direct surveys would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Software can absorb the interface and routine advice, but it does not eliminate accountable procurement, inventory ownership, supplier and territory management, hazardous-material handling, delivery/pickup, credit, returns, warranty support, or urgent local availability. Most year-five quantity should still require a merchant-wholesale operator, although fewer interactions will require a person.

caveats: Watsco's reported margin expansion reflects OEM pricing and scale as well as technology.
Supplier rebates, exclusive territories, and local competition create wide variation.
This excludes demand volume effects and implementation cost.
q.change_evidence: Matched branch-level gross-margin, headcount, win-rate, churn, and price-realization data before and after automation, plus customer-contract and rebate review by account cohort over multiple renewals, would distinguish retained benefit from pass-through.
q.rationale: Benefits can be retained through improved pricing discipline, fewer errors, faster response, better availability, and reduced administrative hiring. However, resale pricing is visible and transactional, customers can compare distributors, manufacturers may distribute directly, and competition is explicitly based partly on price. The estimate assumes part of the implemented gross benefit is competed away or shared through service and pricing rather than fully captured.

rho.bridge: Watsco's scaled deployment shows feasibility in the same distribution activity, including 24/7 digital ordering and AI, but does not measure five-year implementation at LMM wholesalers. I discount its demonstrated frontier for smaller firms' fragmented ERPs, thin product data, change capacity, and need for human technical accountability. This likely biases implementation upward because the proxy is the industry's largest and best-capitalized operator.
caveats: Public-company implementation is not representative of LMM resources.
Digital order penetration is not the same as labor substitution.
Technical advice and regulated-product handling still require escalation and controls.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of 20-50 LMM HVAC distributors measuring pre/post labor hours, exception rates, customer satisfaction, and sustained adoption for AI quoting, order service, replenishment, and AP/AR over 24 months would move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Most exposed workflows use structured orders, pricing records, product catalogs, inventory data, emails, and invoices, so five years is enough to implement assisted quoting, service agents, forecasting, purchasing, and administrative automation. Integration, data cleanup, supplier restrictions, exception handling, cybersecurity, and employee adoption keep implementation materially below full exposure realization.

s5.bridge: A national owner-readiness report cited by Kiplinger says 49% of business owners want to exit within five years, while Census-based reporting says 51% of small-business owners are over 55. These cover broad small-business populations and intentions, not completed qualifying control transfers in HVAC distribution. I approximately halve the exit intention to allow for internal succession, closures, delayed plans, failed processes, and transfers lacking a durable operation. The proxy can overstate actual sale probability because intentions frequently do not close, but Watsco's 72 historical HVAC/R distribution acquisitions show a real strategic-buyer channel that offsets some of that discount.
caveats: National owner surveys are not industry-specific and measure intentions or age, not completed qualifying transfers.
Watsco acquisition history spans decades and includes businesses outside the exact size band.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction counts from BizBuySell, GF Data, Axial, DealStats, and industry brokers for U.S. HVAC/R distributors with $1-10M normalized EBITDA, reconciled to the eligible-firm denominator and tagged by control type, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Aging owners and an active consolidator ecosystem support meaningful availability, but five-year completed control transfer is substantially narrower than stated exit intent. Supplier consent, customer/key-employee retention, working-capital needs, and cyclicality can prevent or postpone otherwise desired sales.

principal_driver: A large aging installed HVAC base creates repeat, time-sensitive replacement and parts demand that rewards local inventory and information-rich service.
principal_weakness: Supplier-controlled territories and transparent transactional pricing can limit both transferability and retention of automation gains.
